section.title: Gatekeepers, Treasurers, and Officials
section.sectionRationale: After the priests and musicians, the Chronicler turns to three more groups of Levites: the gatekeepers who guard every threshold of God's house, the treasurers who administer both the temple's own treasuries and the plunder devoted from David's wars, and the officers and judges appointed over Israel's wider civil affairs. None of them touch the altar directly, yet all are counted as service to the LORD.
section.thesis: David extends the same careful, lot-determined order given to priests and musicians to the gatekeepers who guard every gate of God's house, the treasurers entrusted with the temple's wealth and the plunder devoted from war, and the officers sent out to judge and administer Israel's affairs, showing that guarding a threshold, keeping accounts, and settling disputes are every bit as much service to God as singing His praise or offering His sacrifices.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the gatekeeping families of Korah and Merari numbered and stationed by lot at every gate; the treasurers of the house of God and of the things devoted from battle; and the officers and judges sent out over Israel's wider affairs, east and west of the Jordan.

units.label: The gatekeepers stationed at every gate
units.note: guarding the thresholds of God's house

verses.text: These were the divisions of the {{shaar|gatekeepers}}: From the Korahites: Meshelemiah son of Kore, one of the sons of Asaph.
Meshelemiah had sons: Zechariah the firstborn, Jediael the second, Zebadiah the third, Jathniel the fourth,
Elam the fifth, Jehohanan the sixth, and Eliehoenai the seventh.
And Obed-edom also had sons: Shemaiah the firstborn, Jehozabad the second, Joah the third, Sachar the fourth, Nethanel the fifth,
Ammiel the sixth, Issachar the seventh, and Peullethai the eighth. For God had blessed Obed-edom.
Also to his son Shemaiah were born sons who ruled over their families because they were strong, capable men.
Shemaiah's sons were Othni, Rephael, Obed, and Elzabad; his brothers were Elihu and Semachiah, also capable men.
All these were descendants of Obed-edom; they and their sons and brothers were capable men with strength to do the work, 62 in all from Obed-edom.
Meshelemiah also had sons and brothers who were capable men, 18 in all.
Hosah the Merarite also had sons: Shimri the first (although he was not the firstborn, his father had appointed him as the first),
Hilkiah the second, Tebaliah the third, and Zechariah the fourth. The sons and brothers of Hosah numbered 13 in all.
These divisions of the gatekeepers, through their chief men, had duties for ministering in the house of the LORD, just as their relatives did.
They cast lots for each gate, according to their families, young and old alike.
The lot for the East Gate fell to Shelemiah. Then lots were cast for his son Zechariah, a wise counselor, and the lot for the North Gate fell to him.
The lot for the South Gate fell to Obed-edom, and the lot for the storehouses to his sons.
The lots for the West Gate and the Shallecheth Gate on the ascending highway fell to Shuppim and Hosah. There were guards stationed at every watch.
Each day there were six Levites on the east, four on the north, four on the south, and two pairs at the storehouse.
As for the court on the west, there were four at the highway and two at the court.
These were the divisions of the gatekeepers who were descendants of Korah and Merari.

units.label: The treasurers of the house of God
units.note: the temple's wealth, and war's plunder, devoted to the LORD

verses.text: Now their fellow Levites were in charge of the {{otsar|treasuries}} of the house of God and the treasuries of the dedicated things.
From the descendants of Ladan, who were Gershonites through Ladan and heads of the families of Ladan the Gershonite, were Jehieli,
the sons of Jehieli, Zetham, and his brother Joel. They were in charge of the treasuries of the house of the LORD.
From the Amramites, the Izharites, the Hebronites, and the Uzzielites:
Shebuel, a descendant of Gershom son of Moses, was the officer in charge of the treasuries.
His relatives through Eliezer included Rehabiah his son, Jeshaiah his son, Joram his son, Zichri his son, and Shelomith his son.

This Shelomith and his brothers were in charge of all the treasuries for the things dedicated by King David, by the heads of families who were the commanders of thousands and of hundreds, and by the army commanders.
They had dedicated some of the plunder from their battles to the repair of the house of the LORD.

Everything that had been dedicated by Samuel the seer, Saul son of Kish, Abner son of Ner, and Joab son of Zeruiah, along with everything else that was dedicated, was under the care of Shelomith and his brothers.

units.label: Officers and judges over Israel's affairs
units.note: away from the temple duties, yet still the LORD's service

verses.text: From the Izharites, Chenaniah and his sons had the outside duties as {{sar|officers}} and judges over Israel.
From the Hebronites, Hashabiah and his relatives, 1,700 capable men, had charge of the affairs of Israel west of the Jordan for all the work of the LORD and for the service of the king.

As for the Hebronites, Jerijah was the chief of the Hebronites, according to the genealogies of his ancestors. In the fortieth year of David's reign the records were searched, and strong, capable men were found among the Hebronites at Jazer in Gilead.

Among Jerijah's relatives there were 2,700 capable men who were heads of families. King David appointed them over the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh for every matter pertaining to God and for the affairs of the king.

shaar.gloss: Gate. The gatekeepers, sho'arim, guarded every threshold of God's house, east, north, south, west, and the storehouses, so that access to the sanctuary was controlled with the same careful order given to priest and musician. Guarding a doorway may seem the humblest of tasks, yet the text counts it a ministering duty in the house of the LORD, verse 12.

otsar.gloss: Treasury, storehouse. This chapter names two kinds: the ongoing treasuries of the house of God and its dedicated things, verse 20, and the treasuries of plunder devoted from David's own battles, verse 27, both entrusted to named Levitical families who administer, not merely guard, the wealth given to the LORD.

sar.gloss: Official, commander, chief. Chenaniah and his sons served as sarim, officers, and judges over Israel's outside affairs, verse 29, a reminder that Levites were appointed not only to sacred rites within the temple but also to civil administration and justice among the people, all counted as service to God.

basic.context: Having ordered the priests and musicians, David now organizes three more groups of Levites who never touch the altar directly. The gatekeepers, descended from Korah and Merari, are counted family by family and assigned by lot to guard each of the temple's gates, east, north, south, west, and the storehouses, with guards posted at every watch around the clock. The treasurers oversee two distinct funds: the regular treasuries of the house of God, and a separate store of plunder that David and his commanders had devoted from their wars specifically for the temple's future repair. Finally, some Levites are sent out entirely away from the temple as officers and judges over Israel's wider civil affairs, on both sides of the Jordan, chosen from families whose genealogies were carefully searched to confirm capable men for the task.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical, administrative narrative describing how David organized non-priestly, non-musical Levitical service; it is not a command for churches today to install gatekeepers or treasurers by ancestry. What it teaches is that God does not divide work into sacred and secular, guarding a door, keeping accounts, and settling legal disputes are named alongside singing and sacrificing as service in and for the house of the LORD.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is framed by two forms of guarding: the shaar, the gate, watched around the clock by capable men chosen by lot, and the otsar, the treasury, holding both the temple's own wealth and the plunder David devoted from his wars to its future repair. It closes with Levites serving as sarim, officers, sent entirely outside the temple to judge Israel's affairs, showing the reach of God's ordered service well beyond the sanctuary's walls.
basic.typology: The gatekeepers who guard access to God's house anticipate the deeper truth that access to God Himself is carefully guarded and, in Christ, graciously opened, Hebrews 10:19-22. The treasurers who administer plunder devoted from war point to the pattern of every victory and every resource ultimately belonging to the LORD and returning to His service, fulfilled when Christ leads captivity captive and gives gifts to His church, Ephesians 4:8.
basic.christ: The gatekeepers guarding every threshold of the earthly temple point forward to Christ, who is Himself both the gate and the great High Priest who tore the curtain and opened a new and living way into God's presence, John 10:9 and Hebrews 10:19-20. The treasurers who devoted war's plunder to God's house anticipate the truth that every victory belongs to the Lord and its spoils are rightly returned to His purposes, fulfilled when the ascended Christ gives good gifts to His people, Ephesians 4:7-8. And the officers sent to judge Israel's ordinary affairs, away from the temple, foreshadow the truth that Christ's lordship extends over all of life, not merely its explicitly religious corners.
basic.god: God cares about guarded access to His presence, entrusted wealth, and just administration among His people, appointing capable, vetted men to each. He blesses faithful service in unglamorous places, noting that God had blessed Obed-edom, verse 5, the same man who once housed the ark, 1 Chronicles 13:14, and who now leads sixty-two capable gatekeeping relatives. And He counts civil, judicial work, done for Israel's ordinary affairs away from the sanctuary, as still being for the work of the LORD, verse 30, refusing to shrink His service down to ritual alone.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Chronicles 13:14 tells how the LORD blessed Obed-edom's household when the ark stayed with him, the root of his family's later prominence as gatekeepers here. Psalm 84:10 treasures even being a doorkeeper in the house of God above dwelling in the tents of the wicked. Hebrews 10:19-22 speaks of the new and living way Christ opened into God's presence, once carefully guarded and now freely given. Ephesians 4:7-8 sees Christ's ascension itself as a triumphant giving of gifts, echoing the devoted plunder of this chapter.
basic.oneStory: Guarding a gate, keeping accounts, and settling disputes among Israel's tribes are all folded into the single, sweeping category of service to the LORD in this chapter, preparing the returning exiles, and every reader since, to see no task as too small or too secular to matter to God. All of it anticipates Christ, who is the true gate into God's presence, the one who returns every spoil of His victory to bless His people, and the Lord of every ordinary corner of life.

context.literary: This chapter completes the trio of chapters 24 to 26 devoted to priests, musicians, and now gatekeepers, treasurers, and officers, before chapter 27 turns to purely civil and military structures. The careful, repeated numbering, 62, 18, 13, 1,700, 2,700, mirrors the pattern already set for priests and musicians, extending the same dignity of order to these less visible roles.
context.historical: A functioning temple needed guarded access day and night, and a kingdom that had absorbed plunder from many wars, 1 Chronicles 18 to 20, needed trustworthy stewards to keep dedicated wealth separate from ordinary use until the temple could be built. The note that 'in the fortieth year of David's reign the records were searched,' verse 31, shows this organizing likely happened very near the end of David's life, part of his final, urgent preparations for Solomon and the temple.
context.canonical: Gatekeeping recurs across Scripture as a treasured, if humble, form of service, Psalm 84:10's famous preference for being a doorkeeper in God's house over dwelling among the wicked likely echoes exactly this office. The devoted plunder anticipates the broader biblical pattern of firstfruits and spoils being returned to God, culminating in the New Testament's picture of every good gift, including victory itself, as belonging to and flowing from the Lord, James 1:17 and Ephesians 4:8.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that David's organizing work extended beyond priests and musicians to gatekeepers, treasurers, and civil officers, so that every function needed for a rightly ordered kingdom and temple, guarding, administering wealth, and judging disputes, was seen as legitimate, dignified service to the LORD, not a lesser task beneath the notice of sacred history.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical arrangement of Levitical duties under David; it does not prescribe that modern churches must appoint literal gatekeepers or treasurers by hereditary lot. What is abidingly true is that God values careful stewardship of access, resources, and justice, and considers such work, however unglamorous, to be real ministry.

debates.question: Why does the chapter specifically note that plunder from war, not just ordinary offerings, was devoted to the temple treasury, verses 26-28?

views.label: To show that even the spoils of violence are reclaimed for holy use
views.case: Ancient Near Eastern kings typically kept war plunder for personal enrichment or their own monuments; by devoting it instead to the future house of the LORD, David and his commanders, along with earlier leaders like Samuel and Saul, verse 28, declare that victory itself belongs to God and its fruits rightly return to Him rather than to human glory.

views.label: To trace an unbroken chain of dedicated wealth across Israel's history
views.case: By naming Samuel, Saul, Abner, and Joab alongside David, the text presents a continuous record of devoted plunder spanning multiple generations and leaders, showing this was not a novel practice invented by David but an established pattern of Israel's leadership consistently setting apart war's gains for God's house.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that this careful accounting of devoted plunder reflects a settled conviction that military victory belongs to the LORD and that its spoils are rightly returned to His service rather than kept for human advantage.

debates.question: Why are some Levites, like Chenaniah and Hashabiah, sent entirely away from the temple to serve as officers and judges over Israel's civil affairs, verses 29-32?

views.label: Because all of life, not only ritual, belongs to God's rule
views.case: By deploying trained, capable Levites into civil administration and justice, David embeds the conviction that governing Israel rightly, settling disputes and administering affairs, is itself a form of serving the LORD, not a secular task separate from worship; verse 30 explicitly calls this 'the work of the LORD.'

views.label: Because the Levites' priestly training suited them for impartial justice
views.case: Levites, set apart from tribal land inheritance and trained in the law, were uniquely positioned to serve as impartial judges and administrators without the competing interests of tribal landholders, making them a natural choice for maintaining justice across Israel's scattered tribes, especially in border regions like Gilead, verse 31.

debates.bearing: Either way, the text refuses to confine God's service to the sanctuary; capable, trained servants of the LORD are sent into the ordinary, practical work of justice and administration, and it is called holy work all the same.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter moves through three guarded domains: the shaar, the gate, watched day and night at every entrance to God's house by families chosen through the same fair lot used for priest and musician alike; the otsar, the treasury, holding both the temple's regular wealth and a separate store of plunder that David's own wars had devoted to its future repair; and finally the sarim, officers, Levites like Chenaniah sent entirely outside the sanctuary to judge and administer Israel's affairs, a role the text still calls 'the work of the LORD,' verse 30, refusing to let holiness shrink down to ritual space alone.

typology.type: The gatekeepers guarding every threshold of God's house
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who is Himself the gate and the way opened into God's presence, John 10:9 and Hebrews 10:19-20.

typology.type: The treasuries holding plunder devoted from David's wars
typology.fulfillment: Christ's ascension, in which He leads captivity captive and gives gifts to His people, Ephesians 4:7-8.

typology.type: Levites sent out as officers and judges over Israel's ordinary affairs
typology.fulfillment: The lordship of Christ extending over every sphere of life, not confined to explicitly religious activity, Colossians 3:17.

study.christ: The gatekeepers who guard every threshold of the earthly temple, day and night, at every gate, point forward to Christ, who names Himself the gate for the sheep, John 10:9, and who as the great High Priest tore the curtain guarding God's presence, opening a new and living way for all who trust Him, Hebrews 10:19-20. The treasurers who carefully devoted plunder from David's wars to God's house anticipate the truth that every victory ultimately belongs to the Lord, fulfilled when the ascended Christ, having triumphed over sin and death, gives good gifts to His church, Ephesians 4:7-8. And the Levites sent out to judge Israel's civil affairs, still called 'the work of the LORD' even far from the temple, foreshadow the truth that in Christ, no sphere of ordinary life, work, justice, and administration included, stands outside the reach of worshipful obedience, Colossians 3:17, 23-24.
study.god: God cares about guarded access to His presence, faithful stewardship of dedicated resources, and just administration among His people, and He appoints capable, vetted servants to each task. He remembers and blesses long-term faithfulness, noting again that He had blessed Obed-edom, verse 5, the same man once entrusted with the ark itself, whose household now leads sixty-two capable gatekeepers. And He refuses to let holiness shrink to ritual alone: civil judgment and administration for Israel, done even away from the temple, are still named as the work of the LORD, verse 30, revealing a God whose lordship reaches every corner of ordinary life.

crossReferences.note: The LORD blessed Obed-edom's household when the ark rested there, the root of the honor his family carries as gatekeepers in this chapter.
The psalmist treasures being a doorkeeper in the house of God above dwelling in the tents of the wicked, likely echoing the office described here.
Christ opens a new and living way into God's presence, the access this chapter's gatekeepers so carefully guarded.
Christ's ascension is pictured as leading captivity captive and giving gifts, echoing devoted plunder returned to God's purposes.
Whatever is done, in word or deed, is to be done for the Lord, extending the principle that even civil administration is service to God.

study.oneStory: David's organizing hand reaches even the humblest tasks, guarding a gate, keeping the temple's accounts and its devoted plunder, and judging Israel's ordinary disputes, and calls every one of them service to the LORD. This refusal to separate the sacred from the ordinary points forward to Christ, the true gate into God's presence, the giver of every good gift won in His victory, and the Lord of all of life, under whom no task, however unseen, is beneath the dignity of worship.

quiz.q: How were the gatekeepers assigned to their specific posts at the temple's gates?

options.text: By casting lots for each gate, according to their families, young and old alike.
options.note: Verse 13 states they cast lots for each gate without favoring age or seniority.

options.text: By the personal appointment of Obed-edom, since his family was the largest.
options.note: Obed-edom's family was indeed large and blessed, verse 5, but the text specifies the lot, not personal appointment, decided the posts.

options.text: By birth order alone, with firstborn sons receiving the most prominent gates.
options.note: The text explicitly says the lot was cast for each gate, and even notes elsewhere that a non-firstborn son, Shimri, was appointed first by his father's choice, verse 10, showing birth order was not the ruling principle.

options.text: By family size, with larger families receiving more numerous or prominent posts.
options.note: Family size did shape how many capable men each family supplied to the watch, but the specific gate assignments themselves came by lot, not by size ranking.

quiz.q: What two distinct treasuries does this chapter describe the Levites administering?

options.text: The regular treasuries of the house of God, and a separate treasury of plunder from war devoted to the temple's future repair.
options.note: Verses 20 and 26-27 distinguish the house of God's ongoing treasuries from the specific plunder devoted by David and earlier leaders.

options.text: Only the personal wealth of King David himself.
options.note: The treasuries described belong to the house of God and to dedicated war plunder, not David's private wealth.

options.text: The treasuries of the house of God, and the tithes collected from ordinary Israelite worshippers.
options.note: Ordinary tithes are not the subject named in this chapter; the second treasury specifically holds devoted war plunder, verses 26-27.

options.text: The treasuries of the house of God, which included some wealth gained from David's wars.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but this option blurs together what the text keeps distinct, the regular temple treasuries and the specific, separately administered treasury of devoted war plunder, verses 20 and 26-27.

quiz.q: What was the plunder in the second treasury specifically devoted toward, according to verse 27?

options.text: The repair of the house of the LORD.
options.note: Verse 27 states they dedicated some of the plunder from their battles to the repair of the house of the LORD.

options.text: Paying the wages of David's soldiers.
options.note: The plunder was devoted to the LORD's house, not distributed as military wages.

options.text: Funding future wars against Israel's enemies.
options.note: The purpose given is temple repair and maintenance, not funding further military campaigns.

options.text: Supporting the ongoing needs of the priesthood generally.
options.note: This is close to the temple's purposes broadly, but the text specifically names 'the repair of the house of the LORD' as the devoted plunder's purpose.

quiz.q: What role did Levites like Chenaniah and Hashabiah take on, away from the temple itself?

options.text: They served as officers and judges over Israel's outside affairs, work the text still calls the work of the LORD.
options.note: Verses 29-30 describe their outside duties as officers and judges, explicitly named the work of the LORD.

options.text: They were removed from Levitical service entirely for failing to qualify as gatekeepers.
options.note: The text presents this as a distinct, honored assignment, not a demotion or disqualification.

options.text: They assisted the priests only during the high festivals, otherwise having no ongoing duties.
options.note: Their described role was administering Israel's civil affairs and justice year-round, not simply festival assistance.

options.text: They administered civil affairs specifically for the tribes west of the Jordan, as with Hashabiah's assignment.
options.note: True of Hashabiah in verse 30, but it narrows the picture; Chenaniah's judgeship in verse 29 and other officers appointed east of the Jordan in verse 32 show the assignment was broader than one region.

quiz.q: What does this chapter, taken as a whole, teach about tasks like guarding a gate or keeping accounts?

options.text: That such tasks are genuine, dignified service to the LORD, not lesser work beneath the notice of sacred history.
options.note: The careful naming and numbering of gatekeepers, treasurers, and officers treats their work as real ministry alongside priests and musicians.

options.text: That such tasks are purely secular and unrelated to worship.
options.note: The chapter's entire structure integrates these roles into the LORD's service, explicitly calling civil administration 'the work of the LORD,' verse 30.

options.text: That such tasks matter mainly because they support the priesthood's central sacrificial ministry.
options.note: True in part, since gatekeepers and treasurers do serve the sanctuary's function, but it understates the chapter's point that even work entirely outside the temple, like civil judgment, verse 30, stands on its own as the work of the LORD.

options.text: That such tasks were necessary but spiritually insignificant compared to the priesthood and music described earlier.
options.note: The parallel structure and careful attention given to gatekeepers, treasurers, and officers argues against ranking their spiritual significance below the more visible priestly and musical roles.

questions: Guarding a gate day and night seems unglamorous compared to singing praise or offering sacrifice, yet Scripture counts it real service to God. What unglamorous task has God given you that you might be undervaluing?

God specifically remembered His blessing on Obed-edom, whose household had once cared for the ark and now leads a large family of gatekeepers. Where have you seen God's blessing on a season of faithful, hidden service bear fruit later in a more visible way?

The plunder from war was devoted to repairing God's house rather than kept for personal gain. What 'spoils' in your own life, resources, opportunities, or successes, could you devote more deliberately back to God's purposes?

Levites were sent out to judge and administer Israel's ordinary civil affairs, and the text calls this the work of the LORD. How does this challenge any instinct you have to separate your 'spiritual life' from your everyday work, finances, or civic responsibilities?

Careful records and genealogies were searched to find capable, trustworthy men for these roles. What would it look like for you to steward trust, whether over money, access, or responsibility, with the same integrity this chapter describes?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes that 'on the ascending highway' can also be rendered 'on the upper road,' describing the location of the Shallecheth Gate; the sense of an incline near this gate is retained either way.

The BSB footnote observes that the underlying Hebrew word rendered 'court' is parbar, a term of uncertain precise meaning, occurring twice in this verse and likely referring to a colonnade or open area on the temple's west side.

The BSB footnote notes that the Septuagint supports 'their fellow Levites were in charge of,' while the Hebrew text as it stands reads 'As for the Levites, Ahijah was'; the BSB follows the Septuagint's smoother sense here.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Chronicles 26:1-32. Source em dashes, especially in the summary totals such as '62 in all' and '18 in all,' were converted to commas; no dashes remain in this file.
